John recounts the miracle of feeding the multitude because it relates to the following discussion. Observe the effect this miracle had on the people. Even the common Jews expected the Messiah to come into the world and be a great Prophet. The Pharisees looked down on them as not knowing the law, but they knew more about Him who is the end of the law. Yet, people may acknowledge Christ as that Prophet and still ignore him.

Here were Christ’s disciples performing their duties, and Christ was praying for them; yet they were in distress. There can be dangers and hardships when someone has a relationship with Christ. Clouds and darkness often surround those who follow God's light; they saw Jesus walking on the sea. Even approaches to comfort and deliverance are sometimes misunderstood and become causes of fear. Nothing is more convincing to sinners than that statement, “I am Jesus whom you persecuted,” and nothing is more comforting to believers than this, “I am Jesus whom you love.” If we have received Christ Jesus as Lord, even if the night is dark and the wind is strong, we can find comfort knowing we will reach shore soon.

Instead of answering how he arrived there, Jesus criticized their question. We should put all our effort into seeking salvation through the methods God has provided; yet it must be sought only as a gift from the Son of man. The Father has approved him, proving him to be God. He declared the Son of man to be the Son of God with power.

A constant reliance on faith in Christ is the most important and difficult part of obedience required of us as people seeking salvation. When, by his grace, we are enabled to live a life of faith in the Son of God, good qualities develop, and acceptable service can be done. God, even his Father, who gave their fathers food from heaven to sustain their physical lives, now gives them the true Bread for the salvation of their souls. Coming to Jesus and believing in him mean the same thing. Christ shows that he is the true Bread; he is to the soul what bread is to the body, nourishing and sustaining spiritual life. He is the Bread of God. Bread which the Father gives, which he has made to be the food for our souls. Bread nourishes only through the power of a living body; but Christ himself is living Bread, and nourishes by his own power. The teaching about Christ who died on the cross is now as strengthening and comforting to believers as it ever was. He is the Bread that came down from heaven. It indicates the Divinity of Christ’s nature and authority, also the Divine origin of all good things which flow to us through him. May we say with understanding and sincerity, Lord, always give us this Bread.

The discovery of their guilt, danger, and remedy by the teaching of the Holy Spirit makes people willing and glad to come and to abandon everything that prevents them from seeking salvation. The Father’s will is that none of those who were given to the Son should be rejected or lost by him. No one comes unless Divine grace has subdued and changed his heart; therefore, no one who comes will ever be turned away. The gospel finds no one willing to be saved in the humbling, holy way made known within it; but God draws people with his word and the Holy Spirit; and a person’s duty is to listen and learn—that is, to accept the grace offered and agree to the promise. No one had seen the Father except his beloved Son; and the Jews must expect to be taught by his inner power upon their minds, by his word, and by the ministers he sent among them.

The benefit of manna was small; it only referred to this life. But the living Bread is so excellent that whoever eats it will never die. This bread is Christ’s human nature, which he took to present to the Father as a sacrifice for the sins of the world, to purchase all things pertaining to life and godliness for sinners of every nation who repent and believe in him.

The flesh and blood of the Son of man refer to the Redeemer in human nature; Christ and his crucifixion, and the redemption accomplished by him, with all the precious benefits of redemption: forgiveness of sin, acceptance with God, the way to the throne of grace, the promises of the covenant, and eternal life. These are called the flesh and blood of Christ because they were purchased by his body being broken and his blood shed. Also, because they are nourishment for our souls. Eating this flesh and drinking this blood means believing in Christ. We share in Christ and his benefits through faith. The soul that truly knows its state and needs finds whatever can calm the conscience and promote true holiness in the Redeemer, God manifest in the flesh. Meditating on the cross of Christ gives life to our repentance, love, and gratitude. We live by him, just as our bodies live by food. We live by him, as members depend on the head, branches on the root: because he lives we shall live also.

Christ’s human nature had not previously been in heaven, but being God and man, that wonderful Person was truly said to have come down from heaven. The Messiah's kingdom was not of this world; and they were to understand by faith what he had said about a spiritual reliance on him and his fullness. As without the soul of man the flesh is worthless, so without the quickening Spirit of God all forms of religion are dead and useless. He who made this provision for our souls alone can teach us these things and draw us to Christ that we may live by faith in him. Let us turn to Christ, thankful that it is declared that everyone who is willing to come to him will be welcomed.

When we allow difficult thoughts about the words and actions of Jesus into our minds, we enter temptation, which, if the Lord does not prevent with mercy, will end in turning away. The corrupt and wicked heart of man often uses what is meant for comfort as an opportunity for offense. Our Lord had, in the previous discussion, promised eternal life to his followers; the disciples focused on that clear statement and resolved to stay faithful when others focused on difficult statements and abandoned him. Christ’s teaching is the word of eternal life, so we must live and die by it. If we abandon Christ, we abandon our own blessings. They believed that this Jesus was the Messiah promised to their fathers, the Son of the living God. When we are tempted to backslide or turn away, it is good to remember basic truths and stick to them. And let us always remember our Lord’s searching question: Shall we go away and abandon our Redeemer? He alone can give salvation through forgiveness of sins. This alone brings confidence, comfort, and joy, and drives fear and despair away. It gains the only true happiness in this world and opens a way to the happiness of the next.
